imageCaption: A kitchen mid-transformation: sunlight catching on walls that seem to shift between white and the exact shade of yellow found on Curaçao doors, with Anthony's pegboard hanging where a painting should be and the faint afterimage of Isla visible in a doorway that's already closed.
text:
The house stopped being the temporal garden's house the moment they walked through the probability door.

It was the same space, but wrong in the way that things are wrong when they realize they've been disguised the whole time. The walls were still white, but now they held the memory of being other colors—yellow specifically, the yellow that Aaliyah had seen on doors in every photograph Luisana pretended not to be looking at during her 2 AM research sessions.

"Oh," Luisana said. Her voice sounded like someone had reached directly into her chest and made her speak from there instead of her throat. "Oh, Anthony. Did you—"

"I didn't know," Anthony said quickly. But he was looking at the walls with an expression that suggested he'd maybe always known on some level that things were going to do this. That the house was going to become itself. "I swear I didn't know this was where we were going when I—"

"When you what?" Aaliyah asked, because she was starting to understand that her parents had been having an entire conversation that the rest of the family hadn't been invited to, and that conversation had somehow resulted in an architectural transformation of the space they were standing in.

But Anthony wasn't looking at her. He was looking at the pegboard in his hands. And as Aaliyah watched, one of the miniature license plates—the Curaçao one, she realized, the small blue and white replica that had appeared in his collection exactly three weeks ago despite the fact that they'd never been to Curaçao—began to glow.

Not metaphorically. Actually emit light, as if the idea of Curaçao had decided it had been too quiet and needed to announce itself.

"When I understood that we weren't actually lost," Anthony said slowly. "That the only reason we kept ending up in these glitch zones was because the universe was trying to show us something. And that something wasn't anywhere. That something was with us the whole time. That we just needed to build a space where it could be real."

Elijah had wandered into what the house was apparently deciding was a kitchen now. There were appliances. A stove that looked like it had been borrowed from multiple decades simultaneously. A refrigerator that was humming a melody Aaliyah was almost certain was intentional. 

"There's a window," Elijah announced, "that shows the ocean. But the ocean is in like five different time periods. There's a ship that hasn't been built yet and a sailing technique from 1834 and I think maybe a future where people learned to breathe underwater?"

"That's the threshold state still settling," Isla said. She was in the doorway—the same doorway, Aaliyah realized, where she'd been in the previous house. Where she'd probably always been in every iteration of this house, existing just slightly outside the moments where the family needed her to be elsewhere. "The house is learning which version of itself it's supposed to be. It's still deciding."

"By existing in all of them at once?" Aaliyah asked.

"Until you decide which one is most real," Isla confirmed. "A house, unlike a door, can eventually collapse into a single timeline. But only if the people living in it commit to where they want to be."

Sully had found the back of the house—or what he'd decided was the back, at least. He was lying in a patch of sunlight that was definitely coming from somewhere south-facing, definitely warm, definitely the kind of light that existed in specific latitudes. The dog had tucked his chin onto his paws and was watching the family with the expression of someone who had already made all the decisions and was waiting for everyone else to catch up.

Luisana had moved to the wall where the pegboard should probably go. Anthony hadn't hung it yet—he was still holding it like it was evidence, like it contained proof of something important. But the wall knew. The wall had already decided on its purpose.

"This is Curaçao," Luisana said. It wasn't a question.

"This is the possibility of Curaçao," Isla corrected gently. "This is what a home could be if a family chose to build it here instead of everywhere else. This is what spontaneity looks like when it finally decides to put down roots."

Anthony's hands were shaking slightly. Aaliyah recognized the expression on his face—it was the same expression he'd had when the butterfly had appeared, when he was standing with his grandmother's gift in his hands and understanding that inheritance wasn't always about money or documents. Sometimes it was about permission to become the person you'd been teaching yourself to be all along.

"I need to ask your mother something," he said. And then, before anyone could respond, "Lulu. I need to ask you something."

Luisana turned from the wall. Her eyes were already bright with the kind of tears that came from happiness being too big to contain in normal ways. "You're going to ask me if I want to stay."

"I'm going to ask you if you want to stay," Anthony confirmed.

"And I'm going to say yes," Luisana said. "But only if Aaliyah says yes. Only if Elijah says yes. Only if this is what we're all choosing."

Aaliyah was standing in a kitchen that didn't fully exist yet, watching her parents have a conversation they'd been building toward for seventy-two days and forty-three license plates' worth of distance. She could feel the question underneath the question—not "do you want to stay here" but "do you want to stop running." Not "do you want a house" but "do you want home to be a place instead of just us."

The hard part would have been if she'd had to choose between those things. But she didn't. Because this house, in whatever state of temporal flux it was currently existing in, was clearly both.

"Yes," Elijah said from the kitchen, without anyone asking him anything. "Yes to all of it. Can we have a rock garden? Like an actual garden of rocks that I collect from here and arrange and it's not growing anything but it's also somehow perfect?"

"Yes," Anthony said, laughing in a way that sounded like relief.

"Aaliyah?" Luisana's voice was gentle but certain. "What do you think? Does this make narrative sense to you?"

Aaliyah looked at her family. At Sully in his patch of future sunlight. At her brother already planning a rock garden in a house that wasn't done being itself. At her father holding his collection of everywhere, finally ready to stop collecting. At her mother who had written it all in her journal without planning the ending, just like the rest of them.

"It makes perfect sense," she said. "It's exactly the kind of ending that shouldn't work but absolutely does. It's the only ending that makes sense for people like us."

Isla smiled. It was the first genuinely warm expression Aaliyah had seen on her face. It suggested that healing temporal fractures was emotionally satisfying, but nothing compared to watching a family choose themselves.

"Then I'll stop interfering with the timeline now," Isla said. "You've written something the universe didn't account for. Now you just get to live it."

And with that, she stepped backward through the doorway that was starting to look very much like yellow, and Aaliyah understood with absolute clarity that Isla had never been part of the timeline at all. She'd been part of the house. Part of the garden. Part of the threshold between everything that was broken and everything that was about to be built.

The doorway closed behind her.

And the house, finally certain of its purpose, settled into Curaçao yellow like it had been planning this the entire time.
